toddler: Stockpile at least a one-week supply of diapers, wet wipes, and diaper rash cream
Store extra baby formula, sterilized bottles, and ready-to-eat baby food pouches
Keep a sturdy baby carrier or sling next to your go-bag for hands-free evacuation
Pack a few small, familiar comfort items (pacifier, favorite small toy) to help soothe them during high-stress situations
child: Create physical identification cards with your contact info and attach them to your child's bags or clothing
Involve them in packing their own small, lightweight emergency backpack with clothes and non-perishable snacks
Practice family evacuation drills frequently as a 'game' to reduce fear and build muscle memory
Pack quiet activities (crayons, small books, cards) to keep them occupied and calm at crowded evacuation centers
no_car: Pack a lightweight, backpack-style go-bag instead of heavy bins to ensure you can carry it while walking
Speak with a trusted neighbor who drives to set up a mutual evacuation carpool agreement in advance
Map out and walk your pedestrian evacuation route to the nearest barangay safe zone to check for obstacles
Identify multiple public transit options or local tricycle routes that lead to safety, and keep small cash bills for fare
pregnant: Keep a physical copy of your maternal records, ultrasound results, and OB-GYN's contact info in a waterproof pouch
Stockpile prenatal vitamins, extra hydration packets, and maternity/postpartum pads in your go-bag
Identify a backup hospital or lying-in clinic along your evacuation route in case your primary one is inaccessible
Pack loose, comfortable clothing and sturdy, non-slip shoes for safe and comfortable movement
senior: Register with your local barangay office to ensure you are on their priority assistance list for evacuations
Store a backup pair of prescription reading glasses and extra hearing aid batteries in your emergency kit
Keep a printed, laminated list of all current medications, exact dosages, and emergency contacts in your wallet
Ensure mobility aids (canes, walkers) are kept right next to the bed at all times so they are easy to grab in the dark
disability: Register with the local barangay PWD (Persons with Disability) desk for targeted early warning and evacuation support
Build a 'personal support network' of at least three nearby friends who know how to operate any specialized equipment you use
If using critical electrical medical devices, secure a portable power station or backup battery to keep them running during outages
Label all specialized equipment (wheelchairs, communication devices) with your name, contact info, and return address
chronic_illness: Ask your doctor for an emergency 30-day prescription refill for all essential daily medications
Purchase insulated medical bags or cooling packs if your medication (like insulin) requires strict refrigeration
Wear a medical alert bracelet or carry a highly visible card detailing your exact condition and contraindicated treatments
Stock up on disease-specific emergency supplies (e.g., extra test strips for diabetes, backup rescue inhalers for asthma)
